title: Securing Apps: How Auth works
excerpt: A Layered Guide to Modern Authentication, Authorization, and Web Security

items.title: The Two Pillars: Authentication vs. Authorization

content:
const{Fragment:e,jsx:n,jsxs:t}=arguments[0];function _createMdxContent(i){const r={code:"code",em:"em",h2:"h2",h3:"h3",li:"li",ol:"ol",p:"p",strong:"strong",ul:"ul",...i.components};return t(e,{children:[n(r.p,{children:"How does a website actually know who you are?"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"When you log into a dashboard, check out an online cart, or access a private repository, the application seamlessly recognizes you and serves up your personal data. It feels simple, but under the hood, it is a complex, heavily engineered architecture of trust."}),"
",n(r.p,{children:'In this guide, we are going to build your understanding of modern web security in layers. We will start from the very foundation how we store a simple password and work our way up to the enterprise-grade protocols that power "Login with Google".'}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"1-why-application-security-matters",children:"1. Why Application Security Matters"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["Before we look at the ",n(r.em,{children:"how"}),", we need to understand the ",n(r.em,{children:"why"}),". Security is not a feature you tack onto an application at the end of development; it is a foundational architectural requirement."]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"The internet is hostile. Automated bots, malicious actors, and automated scripts constantly scan web applications for vulnerabilities. The cost of an insecure system is catastrophic—ranging from massive financial penalties and legal liability to the complete destruction of user trust. Think of the massive data breaches where millions of user records are dumped on the dark web; these almost always stem from failures in the authentication or authorization layers."}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"the-two-pillars-authentication-vs-authorization",children:"The Two Pillars: Authentication vs. Authorization"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"At the core of application security are two distinct problems that developers often conflate:"}),"
",t(r.ol,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:n(r.strong,{children:"Who are you?"})}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:n(r.strong,{children:"What are you allowed to do?"})}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"If an attacker can spoof the answer to the first question, or bypass the rules of the second, your entire system is compromised. Let's start with the most basic way we answer the first question: passwords."}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"2-password-hashing-and-storage",children:"2. Password Hashing and Storage"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["Let’s establish a cardinal rule of web development: ",n(r.strong,{children:"Never, under any circumstances, store passwords in plain text in your database."})," If your database is compromised, every single user's account is instantly exposed."]}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["So, how do we store a password without actually storing it? We use ",n(r.strong,{children:"hashing"}),"."]}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"the-mechanics-of-hashing",children:"The Mechanics of Hashing"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Hashing is a one-way mathematical function. You put a string of text (a password) in, and you get a fixed-length string of gibberish out."}),"
",t(r.ul,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:['"password123" ➔ ',n(r.code,{children:"2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824"})]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:'Crucially, you cannot reverse this process. You cannot turn the hash back into "password123".'}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"salt-and-bcrypt",children:"Salt and bcrypt"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"If two users have the same password, their hashes will be identical. Attackers know this and use massive databases of pre-computed hashes (Rainbow Tables) to crack passwords instantly."}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["To fix this, we introduce a ",n(r.strong,{children:"salt"}),"—a random string of characters added to the password ",n(r.em,{children:"before"}),' it is hashed. This ensures that even if two users have the password "123456", their resulting hashes will look completely different in your MongoDB database.']}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["In modern systems, we use algorithms libraries like ",n(r.strong,{children:"bcrypt"}),". Bcrypt automatically handles salting and is intentionally ",n(r.em,{children:"slow"}),'. It incorporates a "work factor" that forces the CPU to spend a fraction of a second computing the hash. This delay is unnoticeable to a user logging in, but it mathematically cripples an attacker trying to guess millions of passwords a second.']}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"the-verification-process",children:"The Verification Process"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"When a user logs in, the flow looks like this:"}),"
",t(r.ol,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"The user types their password."}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"The server pulls the user's stored hash (and salt) from the database."}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"The server runs the submitted password through bcrypt using the stored salt."}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"If the resulting hash matches the stored hash, the user is authenticated."}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"3-authentication-vs-authorization",children:"3. Authentication vs. Authorization"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Now that we know how to verify a password, we must clearly separate our two security pillars."}),"
",t(r.ul,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Authentication (AuthN):"})," Verifying identity. It is the process of proving you are who you say you are."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Authorization (AuthZ):"})," Verifying permissions. It is the process of checking if you have the right to access a specific resource."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"the-airport-analogy",children:"The Airport Analogy"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["Imagine you are at an airport. When you arrive at the security checkpoint, you hand the agent your Passport. The agent checks your face against the photo. This is ",n(r.strong,{children:"Authentication"}),". They have verified your identity."]}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["However, your Passport does not allow you to board a specific plane. For that, you need a Boarding Pass. When you get to the gate, the agent checks your Boarding Pass to see if you are permitted to walk onto Flight 104 to London. This is ",n(r.strong,{children:"Authorization"}),"."]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Applications need both. A user might successfully authenticate (log in), but they shouldn't be authorized to delete another user's profile."}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"4-role-based-access-control-rbac",children:"4. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["When managing authorization in an application, hardcoding rules for every single user is a nightmare. Instead, we use ",n(r.strong,{children:"Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)"}),"."]}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["RBAC sits on a simple premise: Permissions are assigned to ",n(r.em,{children:"Roles"}),", and Roles are assigned to ",n(r.em,{children:"Users"}),"."]}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"designing-the-hierarchy",children:"Designing the Hierarchy"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Imagine building a blogging platform. You might design three roles:"}),"
",t(r.ul,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"User:"})," Can read public posts and edit their own profile."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Moderator:"})," Can do everything a User can do, plus delete inappropriate comments."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Admin:"})," Can do everything a Moderator can do, plus delete user accounts and view system logs."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["When building an API, you implement RBAC at the routing level. For example, in an Express.js backend, you might write a middleware function that intercepts a request to ",n(r.code,{children:"DELETE /api/users/:id"}),". The middleware checks the user's assigned role. If the role does not contain the ",n(r.code,{children:"delete_users"})," permission, the server immediately rejects the request with a ",n(r.code,{children:"403 Forbidden"})," status code."]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"RBAC is highly scalable. If you need to give a team of 50 people the ability to moderate comments, you don't update 50 user profiles; you simply assign them the Moderator role."}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"5-oauth-20-explained",children:"5. OAuth 2.0 Explained"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Passwords and RBAC are great for isolated applications. But what happens when applications need to talk to each other?"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["Imagine you sign up for a new financial budgeting app, and it asks to connect to your bank to read your transaction history. Historically, the app would ask you to type your actual bank username and password into their website. ",n(r.strong,{children:"This is a massive security failure."})," You are giving a third-party app total control over your bank account, hoping they store your password securely and don't drain your funds."]}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"the-valet-key-analogy",children:"The Valet Key Analogy"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["OAuth 2.0 was created to solve this problem. It is an ",n(r.strong,{children:"Authorization"})," protocol."]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Think of OAuth like a valet key for your car. A valet key allows the parking attendant to drive your car, but it won't open the glove box or the trunk."}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"the-authorization-flow",children:"The Authorization Flow"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Here is how OAuth handles the bank scenario safely:"}),"
",t(r.ol,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"The Client Application"})," (the budgeting app) redirects you to the ",n(r.strong,{children:"Authorization Server"})," (your bank's actual website)."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"You log into your bank directly. The budgeting app never sees your password."}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:["The bank asks you: ",n(r.em,{children:'"Do you grant this budgeting app permission to read your transactions?"'})," (This is the Resource Owner granting consent)."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:["If you say yes, the bank redirects you back to the budgeting app, handing the app an ",n(r.strong,{children:"Access Token"}),"."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:'This Access Token is the valet key. The budgeting app can now attach this token to API requests to your bank. The bank sees the token, knows it only has "read-only" permissions, and returns the data. If the budgeting app gets hacked, the hackers only get a limited-scope, easily revokable token, not your bank password.'}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"6-openid-connect-oidc",children:"6. OpenID Connect (OIDC)"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["OAuth 2.0 is incredible, but it has a strict limitation: It was designed ",n(r.em,{children:"only"})," for Authorization (granting access to resources), not for Authentication (logging the user in)."]}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["Developers started misusing OAuth to authenticate users, which led to messy, insecure implementations. To fix this, the industry built ",n(r.strong,{children:"OpenID Connect (OIDC)"})," as a standardized identity layer ",n(r.em,{children:"on top"})," of OAuth 2.0."]}),"
",n(r.h3,{id:"the-id-token",children:"The ID Token"}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["Where OAuth 2.0 gives the application an Access Token (to access an API), OIDC gives the application an ",n(r.strong,{children:"ID Token"})," (usually a JWT - JSON Web Token)."]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"This ID Token is essentially a digital ID card. It contains claims (facts) about the user: their name, email address, profile picture URL, and when they logged in."}),"
",t(r.p,{children:["When you click a button that says ",n(r.strong,{children:'"Login with Google"'})," or ",n(r.strong,{children:'"Continue with GitHub"'}),", you are using OIDC."]}),"
",t(r.ol,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"The app uses OAuth to redirect you to Google."}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"You authenticate with Google."}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"Google returns an ID Token via OIDC to the app."}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",n(r.p,{children:"The app decodes the ID Token, sees your email address, and logs you into their system without ever requiring you to create a local password."}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"7-modern-authentication-architecture",children:"7. Modern Authentication Architecture"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"When you piece all of these concepts together, you get the modern authentication architectures we see in today's SaaS products and enterprise systems."}),"
",t(r.ul,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Traditional Login Systems:"})," Using a local database, bcrypt for hashing, and session cookies. Still valid for simple, standalone applications."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Social Login Systems:"})," Utilizing OIDC to allow users frictionless onboarding via Google, Apple, or GitHub."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Single Sign-On (SSO):"})," Used heavily in enterprise. A company has dozens of internal tools (HR portal, Slack, Jira). Instead of employees maintaining 20 different passwords, the company uses an Identity Provider (like Okta or Azure AD). The employee logs in once, and OIDC/SAML protocols authenticate them across all connected applications instantly."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Modern frontend frameworks (like React) and backend APIs communicate almost entirely through token-based architectures. The frontend holds a short-lived token, sending it via HTTP headers to an API, which validates the token and applies RBAC rules before returning JSON data."}),"
",n(r.h2,{id:"8-security-best-practices",children:"8. Security Best Practices"}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"Understanding the architecture is only half the battle. Implementing it securely requires strict adherence to industry best practices:"}),"
",t(r.ul,{children:["
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):"})," Passwords can be compromised via phishing. MFA (like an authenticator app generating a time-based code) ensures that even if an attacker steals a password, they cannot log in without physical access to the user's device."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Short Token Expiration:"}),' Access tokens should have a short lifespan (e.g., 15 to 60 minutes). If a token is intercepted by a malicious script, the window of opportunity for the attacker is very small. (You pair this with long-lived "Refresh Tokens" stored securely to maintain the user's session).']}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"The Principle of Least Privilege:"})," When designing your RBAC hierarchy or OAuth scopes, give a user or application the absolute minimum amount of access necessary to perform their job."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Protect Sensitive Routes:"})," In modern single-page applications, you must protect routes on ",n(r.em,{children:"both"})," the frontend and the backend. Hiding a button in React is just UX; enforcing the permission check on the backend API is actual security."]}),"
"]}),"
",t(r.li,{children:["
",t(r.p,{children:[n(r.strong,{children:"Never Store Tokens in LocalStorage:"})," For web applications, storing sensitive access tokens in standard LocalStorage leaves them vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks. Utilize HTTP-Only, secure cookies for session management whenever possible."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(r.p,{children:"By mastering these layers—from the math of bcrypt to the architectural flow of OIDC—you transition from merely building web pages to engineering secure, resilient systems that protect your users' digital lives."})]})}return{default:function(e={}){const{wrapper:t}=e.components||{};return t?n(t,{...e,children:n(_createMdxContent,{...e})}):_createMdxContent(e)}};

title: Modern Database Access
excerpt: A comprehensive technical deep dive into database fundamentals, modern ORMs, Prisma, and Drizzle workflows.

items.title: Why data needs to be stored permanently ?

toc.title: 3. The Problem with Raw Database Queries

content:
const{Fragment:e,jsx:n,jsxs:r}=arguments[0];function _createMdxContent(i){const t={code:"code",em:"em",h2:"h2",h3:"h3",li:"li",ol:"ol",p:"p",pre:"pre",strong:"strong",table:"table",tbody:"tbody",td:"td",th:"th",thead:"thead",tr:"tr",ul:"ul",...i.components};return r(e,{children:[n(t.h2,{id:"0-what-are-dbs-fundamentally",children:"0. What are DBs fundamentally?"}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"To understand about modern database access we first need to understand what these databases like MongoDB and PostgreSQL actually do."}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"These MongoDB and PostgreSQL are actually Database Management Systems. They don't store the data as in themselves but else they reduce our work by making it easier to handle the complex logic of safely writing, updating, and indexing information, preventing data corruption when multiple users try to save at once."}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"1-why-applications-need-databases",children:"1. Why Applications Need Databases?"}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"why-data-needs-to-be-stored-permanently-",children:"Why data needs to be stored permanently ?"}),"
",r(t.ul,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Program Volatility:"})," Computer memory (RAM) is temporary. When a program closes or power is lost, RAM is cleared. Permanent storage ensures user profiles, orders, and application states survive."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Historical Tracking:"})," Businesses rely on permanent records for auditing, analytics, and historical trends."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Crash Recovery:"})," Built-in backup mechanisms allow systems to restore historical data if a catastrophic hardware or software failure occurs."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Interconnected Access:"})," Permanent storage acts as a single, centralized source of truth so multiple, distinct apps can reference the same reliable information."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"structured-vs-unstructured-data",children:"Structured vs. Unstructured Data"}),"
",r(t.ul,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Structured Data:"})," Information that obeys a strict, predictable blueprint. An e-commerce ",n(t.strong,{children:"User"})," will ",n(t.em,{children:"always"})," have an email, a join date, and a hashed password. This lives neatly in rows and columns."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Unstructured Data:"})," Information that resists rigid formatting—an uploaded profile picture (JPEG), a 15-second voice note, or the raw text of a blog post. ",n(t.em,{children:"(Modern systems usually store the raw file in an object store like AWS S3, and store the structured URL pointing to that file inside the database)."})]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"example-of-an-ecommerce-store",children:"Example of an Ecommerce store"}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"In any standard commercial platform, your data breaks down into four foundational pillars:"}),"
",r(t.ol,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Users:"})," ",n(t.em,{children:"Who is acting?"})," (Identity, credentials, preferences)"]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Products:"})," ",n(t.em,{children:"What is being acted upon?"})," (SKUs, inventory counts, prices)"]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Orders:"})," ",n(t.em,{children:"The event connecting the two."})," (User X committed to buying Product Y at Timestamp Z)"]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Payments:"})," ",n(t.em,{children:"The verification of the event."})," (Transaction IDs, gateway settlement statuses)"]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"Without a database, these four concepts remain disconnected ideas floating in temporary code. The database binds them into a traceable business reality."}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"2-sql-vs-nosql-databases",children:"2. SQL vs. NoSQL Databases"}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"SQL databases are relational, structured systems that use tables and fixed schemas, while NoSQL databases are non-relational, distributed systems that use flexible formats like documents or key-value pairs."}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"The choice between them depends entirely on the data structure, scaling needs, and consistency requirements."}),"
",r(t.table,{children:[n(t.thead,{children:r(t.tr,{children:[n(t.th,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"Feature"})}),n(t.th,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"SQL Databases"})}),n(t.th,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"NoSQL Databases"})})]})}),r(t.tbody,{children:[r(t.tr,{children:[n(t.td,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"Data Model"})}),n(t.td,{children:"Relational (tables, rows, columns)"}),n(t.td,{children:"Non-relational (documents, key-value, graphs, columns)"})]}),r(t.tr,{children:[n(t.td,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"Schema"})}),n(t.td,{children:"Rigid, predefined, fixed"}),n(t.td,{children:"Dynamic, flexible, schema-less"})]}),r(t.tr,{children:[n(t.td,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"Scalability"})}),n(t.td,{children:"Vertical (scale up CPU/RAM on one server)"}),n(t.td,{children:"Horizontal (scale out by adding more servers)"})]}),r(t.tr,{children:[n(t.td,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"Transactions"})}),r(t.td,{children:["Strict ",n(t.strong,{children:"ACID"})," compliance for data integrity"]}),r(t.td,{children:["Follows ",n(t.strong,{children:"BASE"})," model (Eventual Consistency)"]})]}),r(t.tr,{children:[n(t.td,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"Data Types"})}),n(t.td,{children:"Best suited for highly structured data"}),n(t.td,{children:"Ideal for unstructured or semi-structured data"})]}),r(t.tr,{children:[n(t.td,{children:n(t.strong,{children:"Complex Joins"})}),n(t.td,{children:"Native support using standard SQL syntax"}),n(t.td,{children:"Generally avoided; handled via nested data or app level"})]})]})]}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"3-the-problem-with-raw-database-queries",children:"3. The Problem with Raw Database Queries"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["To talk to a SQL database natively, you write ",n(t.strong,{children:"Structured Query Language (SQL)"}),". For example:"]}),"
",n(t.pre,{children:n(t.code,{className:"language-sql",children:"SELECT users.name, orders.total 
FROM users 
JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id 
WHERE users.email = 'buyer@example.com';
"})}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"While writing raw SQL gives you 100% control over the engine, building an entire enterprise backend this way introduces massive friction:"}),"
",r(t.ol,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Impedance Mismatch:"})," Databases think in ",n(t.em,{children:"Tables and Rows"}),". Programming languages (like TypeScript or Python) think in ",n(t.em,{children:"Objects, Arrays, and Graphs"}),". Translating a flat SQL row into a rich, nested Javascript Object manually for 150 different API endpoints wastes hundreds of engineering hours."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Refactoring Trap:"})," If you decide to rename the database column ",n(t.code,{children:"user_id"})," to ",n(t.code,{children:"customer_id"}),", standard code editors cannot warn you. Your app will simply compile fine, deploy to production, and crash the moment a user hits that specific endpoint."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Security Vulnerabilities:"})," Hand-writing raw SQL strings makes developers prone to ",n(t.strong,{children:"SQL Injection"}),", where a malicious user types ",n(t.code,{children:'"; DROP TABLE users;--'})," into a login box and tricks the database into executing it."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"4-what-is-an-orm",children:"4. What is an ORM?"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["An ",n(t.strong,{children:"ORM (Object-Relational Mapper)"})," is a productivity bridge. It sits directly between your application code and your database driver."]}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"Instead of writing a raw SQL string, you call a method in your native programming language:"}),"
",n(t.pre,{children:n(t.code,{className:"language-javascript",children:"// The ORM translates this Javascript into SQL
const user = await db.user.findUnique({ where: { email: 'buyer@example.com' } })
"})}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"the-reality",children:"The Reality"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["ORMs are ",n(t.strong,{children:"tools, not magic"}),"."]}),"
",r(t.ul,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Gain:"})," You get massive developer velocity, auto-completed code, automatic sanitization against hackers, and unified type safety."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Cost:"})," You lose a layer of transparency. Poorly configured ORMs are notorious for the ",n(t.strong,{children:'"N+1 Query Problem"'}),"—where asking an ORM to fetch 100 blog posts and their respective authors accidentally triggers 101 separate database hits behind the scenes instead of 1 single optimized SQL ",n(t.code,{children:"JOIN"}),"."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"5-understanding-prisma",children:"5. Understanding Prisma"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["Prisma popularized the ",n(t.strong,{children:"Schema-First"})," ORM wave in the TypeScript ecosystem."]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"the-philosophy",children:"The Philosophy"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["With Prisma, you do not write database tables, nor do you write TypeScript interfaces for your data. You write a single, custom file called ",n(t.code,{children:"schema.prisma"}),":"]}),"
",n(t.pre,{children:n(t.code,{className:"language-plaintext",children:"model User {
  id     Int     @id @default(autoincrement())
  email  String  @unique
  orders Order[]
}
"})}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["When you save this file, Prisma's internal compiler reads it and ",n(t.strong,{children:"auto-generates a bespoke, hyper-tailored TypeScript client"})," inside your ",n(t.code,{children:"node_modules"}),"."]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"the-developer-experience",children:"The Developer Experience"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["Prisma acts like an over-protective pair of guardrails. If you try to query ",n(t.code,{children:"db.user.findMany({ where: { emial: '...' } })"}),", your code editor will throw a bright red underline instantly because it knows ",n(t.code,{children:"emial"})," doesn't exist on the blueprint it generated. It also ships with ",n(t.strong,{children:"Prisma Studio"}),", a local spreadsheet GUI that lets non-technical teammates inspect live data safely."]}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"6-understanding-drizzle",children:"6. Understanding Drizzle"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["If Prisma is an automatic transmission luxury sedan, ",n(t.strong,{children:"Drizzle"})," is a lightweight, manual track car."]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"the-philosophy-1",children:"The Philosophy"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["Drizzle operates on a ",n(t.strong,{children:"SQL-First"})," mandate: ",n(t.em,{children:"“If you know SQL, you already know Drizzle.”"})," Instead of inventing a custom schema language like Prisma, Drizzle defines tables directly in 100% native TypeScript:"]}),"
",n(t.pre,{children:n(t.code,{className:"language-typescript",children:"export const users = pgTable('users', {
  id: serial('id').primaryKey(),
  email: text('email').notNull().unique(),
});
"})}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"the-architecture",children:"The Architecture"}),"
",n(t.p,{children:'Drizzle does not generate a heavy Rust-engine binary client behind the scenes. It is essentially a thin, zero-overhead type-checker that sits right on top of standard SQL drivers. When you write a Drizzle query, the syntax deliberately mirrors exact SQL syntax, meaning there is practically zero "ORM translation tax" occurring at runtime.'}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"7-prisma-vs-drizzle",children:"7. Prisma vs. Drizzle"}),"
",r("table",{style:{minWidth:"75px"},children:[r("colgroup",{children:[n("col",{style:{minWidth:"25px"}}),n("col",{style:{minWidth:"25px"}}),n("col",{style:{minWidth:"25px"}})]}),r("tbody",{children:[r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Feature Metric"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Prisma ORM"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Drizzle ORM"})})})]}),r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Primary Philosophy"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Abstraction-First (Hide the SQL)"})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"SQL-First (Embrace the SQL)"})})]}),r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Learning Curve"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Extremely low for beginners"})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Requires solid fundamental SQL knowledge"})})]}),r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Runtime Engine"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Heavy (Relies on a compiled Rust binary)"})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Ultra-lightweight (Pure JS/TS functions)"})})]}),r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Serverless / Edge"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Requires external proxies (Prisma Accelerate)"})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:r("p",{children:[n("strong",{children:"Native"})," (Runs instantly on Cloudflare/Vercel)"]})})]}),r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Type Safety"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:r("p",{children:["Generated from ",n("code",{children:".prisma"})," schema"]})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Inferred directly from TypeScript code"})})]}),r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Query Control"})})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"Harder to optimize complex edge-case joins"})}),n("td",{colSpan:1,rowSpan:1,children:n("p",{children:"1:1 control over the generated SQL"})})]})]})]}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"8-database-migrations",children:"8. Database Migrations"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["Imagine your live app has 50,000 registered users. You realize your ",n(t.code,{children:"Users"})," table needs a ",n(t.code,{children:"phone_number"})," column. You cannot simply log into the production database GUI and click ",n(t.em,{children:'"Add Column"'}),". If a user tries to register at the exact second you alter the live table structure, the transaction will crash."]}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["Migrations are ",n(t.strong,{children:"Version Control (Git) for your database structure."})]}),"
",r(t.ol,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Delta:"})," You update your code schema to include ",n(t.code,{children:"phone_number"}),"."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Generation:"})," You run a CLI command. The tool looks at your ",n(t.em,{children:"old"})," schema, looks at your ",n(t.em,{children:"new"})," schema, and writes a permanent, timestamped file (e.g., ",n(t.code,{children:"20260627_add_phone.sql"}),") containing the exact safe SQL instructions: ",n(t.code,{children:'ALTER TABLE "User" ADD COLUMN "phone_number" TEXT;'}),"."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Execution:"})," When you deploy your app to production, the server checks a hidden internal table to see which migration files have already been run, and sequentially executes any new ones."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["If a deployment breaks production, ",n(t.strong,{children:"you roll back the migration"}),", safely reverting the database structure to its previous historical state."]}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"9-designing-data-models-erds",children:"9. Designing Data Models (ERDs)"}),"
",r(t.p,{children:["Before writing code, architects map systems visually using ",n(t.strong,{children:"Entity Relationship Diagrams"}),". Relationships define how disparate data tables communicate."]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"one-to-one-1--1",children:"One-to-One (1 : 1)"}),"
",r(t.ul,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Concept:"})," Entity A belongs to strictly one Entity B."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Real-world example:"})," A ",n(t.code,{children:"User"})," and their ",n(t.code,{children:"IdentityVerification"})," profile. A human user only has one driver's license upload; that license belongs to one human."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"one-to-many-1--n",children:"One-to-Many (1 : N)"}),"
",r(t.ul,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Concept:"})," Entity A can own multiple instances of Entity B, but Entity B belongs to only one Entity A."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Real-world example:"})," A ",n(t.code,{children:"Blogging Platform"}),". One ",n(t.strong,{children:"Author"})," can write 40 different ",n(t.strong,{children:"Articles"}),". However, any single Article belongs to strictly one primary Author."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.h3,{id:"many-to-many-n--m",children:"Many-to-Many (N : M)"}),"
",r(t.ul,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"The Concept:"})," Entity A can link to many Entity Bs, ",n(t.em,{children:"and"})," Entity B can link to many Entity As."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Real-world example:"})," An ",n(t.code,{children:"Order"})," and ",n(t.code,{children:"Products"}),". Order #101 contains a Tshirt and a Hat. The Tshirt is also inside Order #102, #108, and #400."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.em,{children:"The Engineering Trick:"})," Relational databases cannot physically draw a Many-to-Many line directly. Architects resolve this by inserting a ",n(t.strong,{children:"Join Table"})," (often called ",n(t.code,{children:"OrderItems"}),") in the middle, breaking it into two clean ",n(t.em,{children:"One-to-Many"})," relationships."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(t.h2,{id:"10-choosing-the-right-tool",children:"10. Choosing the Right Tool"}),"
",n(t.p,{children:"When sitting down to architect a new system, apply this pragmatic heuristic:"}),"
",r(t.ul,{children:["
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Choose Prisma if:"})," You are building a standard monolithic web application (Next.js, NestJS, Express), you are working with a cross-functional team of varying backend experience levels, or your primary startup goal is ",n(t.strong,{children:"maximum velocity to reach an MVP"}),". The safety net of Prisma's DX is unmatched for rapid iteration."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(t.li,{children:["
",r(t.p,{children:[n(t.strong,{children:"Choose Drizzle if:"})," You are building on high-concurrency ",n(t.strong,{children:"Serverless / Edge infrastructure"})," (Cloudflare Workers, Bun, Deno), your app relies on deeply complex, highly customized reporting queries, or you are an experienced engineering team that refuses to let an abstraction layer dictate your database execution plans."]}),"
"]}),"
"]})]})}return{default:function(e={}){const{wrapper:r}=e.components||{};return r?n(r,{...e,children:n(_createMdxContent,{...e})}):_createMdxContent(e)}};

title: REST API Design Made Simple with Express.js
excerpt: A practical guide to REST APIs, covering HTTP methods, CRUD operations, and Express.js implementation.

title: The Architecture of Realtime Systems
excerpt: Explore the protocols and patterns powering instant modern web applications

toc.title: Realtime Systems: Polling, WebSockets, SSE, and Pub/Sub

items.title: 3. Polling: The "Are We There Yet?" Approach

content:
const{Fragment:e,jsx:n,jsxs:r}=arguments[0];function _createMdxContent(t){const i={a:"a",code:"code",em:"em",h1:"h1",h2:"h2",h3:"h3",li:"li",ol:"ol",p:"p",strong:"strong",ul:"ul",...t.components};return r(e,{children:[n(i.h1,{id:"realtime-systems-polling-websockets-sse-and-pubsub",children:"Realtime Systems: Polling, WebSockets, SSE, and Pub/Sub"}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"How does WhatsApp deliver a message instantly?"}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"When you hit "send," the text doesn't just float in the ether waiting for your friend's phone to check for new messages. It appears on their screen in milliseconds. In a world where we expect live sports scores to update seamlessly, Uber drivers to move smoothly on a map, and collaborative docs to show keystrokes in real-time, the traditional rules of web communication simply aren't enough."}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"In this guide, we are going to tear down the architecture of realtime systems. We will start with the fundamental problem of traditional HTTP, explore the hacks developers used to get around it, and eventually unpack the modern protocols and architectures—like WebSockets and Pub/Sub—that power the modern web."}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"1-what-makes-a-system-realtime",children:'1. What Makes a System "Realtime"?'}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["In software engineering, ",n(i.strong,{children:"realtime"}),' means a system guarantees a response within a strict time constraint—often milliseconds. However, in web development, when we say "realtime," we are usually talking about ',n(i.strong,{children:"near realtime"}),". We don't mean the microsecond precision required to fire a rocket engine; we mean human-perceptible instantaneity."]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Modern applications demand this near-instant communication. If you are building a modern web app, you will inevitably encounter these use cases:"}),"
",r(i.ul,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Chat Applications:"})," WhatsApp, Slack, or Discord."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Live Dashboards:"})," Stock market tickers, server health monitors, or IoT sensor data."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Ride Tracking:"})," Watching your cab navigate city traffic on a map."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Collaborative Editing:"})," Google Docs or Figma, where multiple cursors dance across the screen simultaneously."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"2-traditional-request-response-communication",children:"2. Traditional Request-Response Communication"}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["To understand why realtime systems are complex, we have to look at how the web was originally built. The web runs on HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol), which follows a strict ",n(i.strong,{children:"Request-Response"})," model."]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Think of it like ordering food at a restaurant. You (the Client) ask the waiter (the Server) for a burger. The waiter goes to the kitchen, gets the burger, and brings it back. The transaction is complete. The waiter will not bring you more food unless you explicitly ask for it again."}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["![](",n(i.a,{href:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/f11e9fc5-02f8-4c7b-8695-bbfe46f4621b.png",children:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/f11e9fc5-02f8-4c7b-8695-bbfe46f4621b.png"}),' align="center")']}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"This is perfect for loading a static webpage or fetching a user profile. But it completely fails for realtime features. If you are waiting for a chat message, your browser has no way of knowing when the server receives it. The server cannot push the message to you; it has to wait for you to ask."}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"So, how do we solve this without changing the fundamental rules of the web? We start with a brute-force approach."}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"3-polling-the-are-we-there-yet-approach",children:'3. Polling: The "Are We There Yet?" Approach'}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["If the server can't tell us when there is new data, the simplest solution is to just keep asking. This is called ",n(i.strong,{children:"Polling"})," (or Short Polling)."]}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["You set up a timer on the frontend (using something like ",n(i.code,{children:"setInterval"})," in JavaScript) to send an HTTP request to the server every 5 seconds."]}),"
",r(i.ul,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.em,{children:"Client:"}),' "Any new messages?"']}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.em,{children:"Server:"}),' "No."']}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",n(i.p,{children:n(i.em,{children:"(5 seconds later)"})}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.em,{children:"Client:"}),' "Any new messages?"']}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.em,{children:"Server:"}),' "Yes, here is one."']}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(i.h3,{id:"the-tradeoffs-of-polling",children:"The Tradeoffs of Polling"}),"
",r(i.ul,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Advantages:"})," It is incredibly simple to implement. It uses standard HTTP routing and works flawlessly across every browser and network firewall in existence."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Disadvantages:"})," It is horribly inefficient. If you have 10,000 users polling your API every 5 seconds, that is 2,000 requests per second hitting your backend—and 99% of those responses will just be empty data. It wastes bandwidth, drains mobile batteries, and overloads your servers."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Polling is only sufficient when data changes predictably and infrequently, or when building internal dashboards where server load isn't a massive concern."}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"4-long-polling-holding-the-line",children:"4. Long Polling: Holding the Line"}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["Developers realized that Short Polling was killing their servers, so they invented a clever hack: ",n(i.strong,{children:"Long Polling"}),"."]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:'Instead of the server immediately responding with "No data," the server holds onto the request and keeps the connection open.'}),"
",r(i.ol,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",n(i.p,{children:'The client sends a request: "Any new messages?"'}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:["The server receives the request but ",n(i.em,{children:"does not respond immediately"}),"."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",n(i.p,{children:"The connection hangs in suspense."}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",n(i.p,{children:"The moment a new message arrives in the database, the server fires the response back to the client."}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:["As soon as the client gets the response, it immediately opens a ",n(i.em,{children:"new"})," Long Polling request."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["![](",n(i.a,{href:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/15ef4730-468c-4eab-9a05-ae70a1463022.png",children:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/15ef4730-468c-4eab-9a05-ae70a1463022.png"}),' align="center")']}),"
",n(i.h3,{id:"the-tradeoffs-of-long-polling",children:"The Tradeoffs of Long Polling"}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Long polling dramatically reduces the number of empty requests hitting your API. However, holding open thousands of HTTP connections consumes server memory. It was a brilliant stopgap for early chat applications, but it was still a hack on top of a protocol that wasn't designed for it."}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"5-server-sent-events-sse-the-one-way-street",children:"5. Server-Sent Events (SSE): The One-Way Street"}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["Eventually, browsers introduced native APIs to handle realtime streams cleanly. ",n(i.strong,{children:"Server-Sent Events (SSE)"})," is a standard allowing a browser to receive an automatic stream of updates from a server via a single, long-lasting HTTP connection."]}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["Unlike Polling, SSE is a ",n(i.strong,{children:"one-way communication model"}),". The server pushes data to the client, but the client cannot use that same connection to send data back."]}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["If you are building an Express.js backend, implementing SSE is as simple as setting the ",n(i.code,{children:"Content-Type"})," header to ",n(i.code,{children:"text/event-stream"})," and writing data to the response object periodically."]}),"
",n(i.h3,{id:"the-tradeoffs-of-sse",children:"The Tradeoffs of SSE"}),"
",r(i.ul,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Advantages:"})," It uses standard HTTP, so it traverses firewalls easily. It natively supports automatic reconnections and event IDs."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Limitations:"})," It is strictly unidirectional (Server → Client). Browsers also historically limit the number of open SSE connections (usually around 6 per domain)."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"When to use:"})," SSE is the absolute best tool for live sports scores, real-time news feeds, or social media notification counters. You don't need a heavy bidirectional protocol if the user is just reading data."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"6-websockets-the-bidirectional-superhighway",children:"6. WebSockets: The Bidirectional Superhighway"}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["If you want to build a high-performance chat application or a multiplayer game, you need data to flow in both directions instantly. Enter ",n(i.strong,{children:"WebSockets"}),"."]}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["WebSockets are not HTTP. They represent an entirely different protocol (starting with ",n(i.code,{children:"ws://"})," or ",n(i.code,{children:"wss://"}),")."]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:'The connection lifecycle starts with an "HTTP Handshake." The client asks the server to upgrade the connection from HTTP to WebSockets. If the server agrees, the HTTP connection is severed, and a persistent, bidirectional TCP connection is established.'}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["![](",n(i.a,{href:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/1d347d21-c486-4885-b50f-2c6ee6b909a6.png",children:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/1d347d21-c486-4885-b50f-2c6ee6b909a6.png"}),' align="center")']}),"
",n(i.h3,{id:"why-websockets-win-for-chat",children:"Why WebSockets Win for Chat"}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"With a WebSocket, the server and the client can push messages to each other at the exact same time without the overhead of HTTP headers, cookies, or request routing. The performance benefits are staggering. Latency drops to milliseconds, making it the industry standard for anything requiring high-frequency interaction."}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"7-pubsub-systems-the-architectural-glue",children:"7. Pub/Sub Systems: The Architectural Glue"}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["So far, we have talked about protocols—how the client talks to a server. But what happens inside your backend when you have millions of users? You need a ",n(i.strong,{children:"Publish-Subscribe (Pub/Sub)"})," architecture."]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Pub/Sub is a system design pattern that decouples the application sending a message (the Producer) from the application receiving it (the Consumer)."}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Imagine a chat room. When you send a message, your WebSocket server doesn't loop through every user in the database to see who should get it. Instead:"}),"
",r(i.ol,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:["Your server ",n(i.strong,{children:"Publishes"}),' the message to a specific "Topic" (e.g., ',n(i.code,{children:"chatroom_54"}),")."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:["Any user in that chat room has already ",n(i.strong,{children:"Subscribed"})," to ",n(i.code,{children:"chatroom_54"}),"."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",n(i.p,{children:"The Pub/Sub system (often handled by tools like Redis or Apache Kafka) instantly routes the message to all active subscribers."}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["![](",n(i.a,{href:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/2893275b-f4e0-4092-a993-a06bf5957db0.png",children:"https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69501eadecd9fff694a8b6d8/2893275b-f4e0-4092-a993-a06bf5957db0.png"}),' align="center")']}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Pub/Sub is the engine that allows event-driven architectures to scale. It ensures that your chat server doesn't crash trying to figure out where to route data."}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"8-comparing-all-approaches",children:"8. Comparing All Approaches"}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Understanding when to use which protocol is the hallmark of a strong system designer."}),"
",r("table",{style:"min-width: 125px;",children:[r("colgroup",{children:[n("col",{style:"min-width: 25px;"}),n("col",{style:"min-width: 25px;"}),n("col",{style:"min-width: 25px;"}),n("col",{style:"min-width: 25px;"}),n("col",{style:"min-width: 25px;"})]}),r("tbody",{children:[r("tr",{children:[n("td",{colspan:"1",rowspan:"1",children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Feature"})})}),n("td",{colspan:"1",rowspan:"1",children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Polling"})})}),n("td",{colspan:"1",rowspan:"1",children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"Long Polling"})})}),n("td",{colspan:"1",rowspan:"1",children:n("p",{children:n("strong",{children:"SSE (Server-Sent ",n(i.h2,{id:"9-building-realtime-systems-at-scale",children:"9. Building Realtime Systems at Scale"}),"
",r(i.p,{children:["Getting a WebSocket to work on ",n(i.code,{children:"localhost"})," is easy. Scaling it to 100,000 concurrent users is a distributed systems nightmare."]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"When you scale horizontally, you put a Load Balancer in front of multiple backend servers. But WebSockets are stateful. If User A connects to Server 1, and User B connects to Server 2, how do they chat? Server 1 doesn't know User B exists."}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"To solve this, modern architectures combine protocols and patterns:"}),"
",r(i.ul,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Sticky Sessions:"})," You configure your load balancer to ensure a user's requests always route to the same server they initiated their WebSocket on."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"The Pub/Sub Backplane:"})," You connect Server 1 and Server 2 to a centralized Redis Pub/Sub instance. When User A messages User B, Server 1 publishes the message to Redis. Redis broadcasts it to Server 2, which then pushes it down the WebSocket to User B."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(i.h2,{id:"10-choosing-the-right-approach",children:"10. Choosing the Right Approach"}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"Do not default to WebSockets for everything just because they are fast. Engineering is about tradeoffs."}),"
",r(i.ul,{children:["
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Live Sports Scores / Stock Dashboards:"})," Use ",n(i.strong,{children:"SSE"}),". The data only flows one way, and SSE is vastly simpler to implement and debug than WebSockets."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Chat Applications & Collaborative Apps:"})," Use ",n(i.strong,{children:"WebSockets"}),". The bidirectional, low-latency requirements make this non-negotiable."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Background Tasks & Email Processing:"})," Use standard ",n(i.strong,{children:"Polling"}),". If a report takes 5 minutes to generate, checking an endpoint every 15 seconds is perfectly fine."]}),"
"]}),"
",r(i.li,{children:["
",r(i.p,{children:[n(i.strong,{children:"Microservices Communication:"})," Use ",n(i.strong,{children:"Pub/Sub"})," (Kafka/RabbitMQ) to decouple your backend services and ensure reliable message delivery between your own internal systems."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",n(i.p,{children:"By understanding the progression from Polling to WebSockets, and layering Pub/Sub underneath it all, you stop hacking around HTTP constraints and start designing systems that feel truly alive."})]})}return{default:function(e={}){const{wrapper:r}=e.components||{};return r?n(r,{...e,children:n(_createMdxContent,{...e})}):_createMdxContent(e)}};
